coredata.dc:title: Visible-light photopolymerization activated by nanocarbon materials as photocatalysts
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology C: Photochemistry Reviews
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Ke
Zhang, Yijun
Zhu, Di
Peng, Xiaotong
Zhang, Jing
Gong, Tao
Ma, Ming
Xiao, Pu
coredata.dc:description: Although numerous photocatalysts, such as organic compounds and metallic complexes, have been systematically designed and synthesized for visible light-induced photopolymerization, their disadvantages, such as toxicity and poor water dispersibility, cannot be neglected in practical production. Hence, high-performance photoinitiators based on nanomaterials, such as carbon dots and carbon nitrides, have recently been reported due to their favorable characteristics of eco-friendliness, high biocompatibility, etc. In this review, carbon-family-based photocatalysts reported in the last five years are thoroughly discussed by the classifications in terms of their photoinitiation mechanisms, such as photoinduced electron transfer-reversible addition-fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization (PET-RAFT), atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), cationic polymerization (CP) and free radical polymerization (FRP). Carbon family materials play significant roles in the applications of visible light-induced photopolymerization, such as 3D printing and hydrogel materials fabrication. Specifically, they can not only initiate photopolymerization but also endow products with specific chemical or physical properties, e.g., nontoxic, economical, and facile synthesis routes. Finally, reports on newly developed carbon-family nanomaterials, such as silicon quantum dots, are introduced and discussed as perspectives for photopolymerization, which could inspire researchers in the relevant fields.

head.ce:title: Visible-light photopolymerization activated by nanocarbon materials as photocatalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although numerous photocatalysts, such as organic compounds and metallic complexes, have been systematically designed and synthesized for visible light-induced photopolymerization, their disadvantages, such as toxicity and poor water dispersibility, cannot be neglected in practical production. Hence, high-performance photoinitiators based on nanomaterials, such as carbon dots and carbon nitrides, have recently been reported due to their favorable characteristics of eco-friendliness, high biocompatibility, etc. In this review, carbon-family-based photocatalysts reported in the last five years are thoroughly discussed by the classifications in terms of their photoinitiation mechanisms, such as photoinduced electron transfer-reversible addition-fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization (PET-RAFT), atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), cationic polymerization (CP) and free radical polymerization (FRP). Carbon family materials play significant roles in the applications of visible light-induced photopolymerization, such as 3D printing and hydrogel materials fabrication. Specifically, they can not only initiate photopolymerization but also endow products with specific chemical or physical properties, e.g., nontoxic, economical, and facile synthesis routes. Finally, reports on newly developed carbon-family nanomaterials, such as silicon quantum dots, are introduced and discussed as perspectives for photopolymerization, which could inspire researchers in the relevant fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a highly efficient process under mild conditions, photopolymerization is of great importance in many fields, such as optical technologies [1–5], microelectronics [6–9], drug delivery [10,11], additive manufacturing [12,13], and solar fuels [14], exhibiting advantages of low cost, safety, energy savings, environmental protection, etc [15,16]. During the photopolymerization process,the wavelength and intensity of the light source significantly affect the performance of the photopolymerization processes; hence, it is crucial to select appropriate light sources emitting in either ultraviolet (UV), visible, near-infrared (NIR), or far-red ranges to achieve outstanding performance [17,18]. To date, many developments in photopolymerization have been achieved under sunlight irradiation due to its specific characteristics, such as being eco-friendly, human-safe and limitless, which are mainly attributed to the visible component of sunlight [12,19,20]. Moreover, visible light also plays a significant roles in photopolymerization, which can be achieved with the assistance of visible-light-sensitive photocatalysts (PCs) or photoinitiators (PIs). In recent decades, numerous PCs (or PIs), comprising organic compounds, copper complexes, etc., have been investigated by research groups worldwide to achieve effective photopolymerization under visible-light irradiation of artificial or natural light sources [21–25], and these studies have mainly focused on the structural design, synthesis through structural modifications, and improvement of spectroscopic properties.

One of the main strategies to achieve high-performance photopolymerization is to develop excellent PCs whose light absorption can fully adapt to the emission wavelengths of artificial light sources [26–30]. With the increasing awareness of environmental protection, the development of “green” PCs to conduct metal-free [31,32] and oxygen-tolerant polymerization has gained great interests [33–35] Fortunately, remarkable photophysical/photochemical properties, such as high extinction coefficients (>105 M−1 cm−1), broad absorption spectra, and tunable band gaps and energy levels, have been observed from many nanomaterials and reported in literature [36–38]. In particular, it has been reported that thiol-ene polymerization can be successfully initiated using the perovskite quantum dots (CsPbBr3 QDs) under the irradiation of LED@ 410 nm and 530 nm. By further incorporating the prepolymer systems with co-initiators, photopolymerization under LED@ 640 nm irradiation could also be achieved [39]. This research proved the potential of such light harvesters being used as the efficient visible light-sensitive initiators in photopolymerization. Similarly, nanocarbon materials, e.g. carbon dots (CDs) and carbon nitrides (CNs), named as nanocarbon-based PCs (or PIs) in the context, are also rational to be introduced into photopolymerization, which has been utilized extensively in many fields according to previous studies, such as hydrogel formation, additive manufacturing, H2O2 production, light-emitting diodes, and solar cells [40–48]. As expected, the excellent characteristics of nanocarbon-based PCs have been recognized, comprising tunability, good excitation under blue/UV light, high quantum yields, low energy activation, and high water-dispersion [49–51]. Especially, unlike traditional QDs such as CdSe/ZnS or perovskites with heavy metals, CDs and CNs are environment-friendly, less toxic, and can be prepared from low cost raw materials in a facile and economical approach. Hence, nanocarbon materials is likely to role as stronger competitors to traditional QDs in the presence of live cells, where the heavy metals limit their further applications in bio-fields, including tissue engineering, in vivo bioimaging, and nanomedicine [52]. However, the composition and structure of CDs or CNs with ambiguities leads to their relatively low and unclear quantum yields, compared to that of organic dyes and QDs which has been well-defined, thus the synthetic routes are urgent to be developed to produce nanocarbon materials with reproducibility and clear structures, as well as the improved quantum yields.

Currently, although nanocarbon-based materials seem to be promising alternatives as PCs (or PIs) to organic PCs, metal complexes, and even traditional QDs in photopolymerization, there are few reviews summarizing nanocarbon materials as PCs (or PIs) of photopolymerization. In this review, carbon-based nanomaterials developed in the last five years, especially CDs and carbon nitrides, which have been used as efficient PCs for visible-light-induced photopolymerization, are systematically discussed. These intense research efforts by chemists and material scientists can significantly contribute to exploring the reaction mechanisms of visible-light-induced photopolymerization under the catalysis of carbon-based nanomaterials and demonstrate the rapid expansion of their applications. In Scheme 1, we summarize and classify the carbon-family material-mediated photopolymerization with their practical applications and provide a future-oriented perspective.

Photopolymerization has been successfully incorporated into various polymerization systems according to their mechanisms during the polymerization process, including cationic polymerization (CP), nitroxide-mediated radical polymerization (NMP), free radical polymerization (FRP), ring-opening polymerization, atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), and reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization. In the past decade, organic dyes and metal complexes have been over-explored and well-discussed as type I or type II PIs (or PCs), e.g., benzophenone [53,54] indane-1,3-dione [55], nitrocarbazole-based oxime esters [56], and bis-chalcones 57]). Although the use of PIs/PCs in photoinduced polymerization is an efficient and attractive approach, comprehension of the initiation mechanisms of these materials may be relatively complex and confusing for researchers beyond this area.

Briefly, for type I PIs, the photopolymerization process proceeds according to the following mechanism [58]:
(r1) PI → PI* (hν) → R• + R•

For type II PIs, the photopolymerization process proceeds according to the following mechanism [58]:
(r2) PI → PI* (hν) + DH → RH• + D•

Organic dyes (or metal complexes) as type II PIs are usually combined with bis[4-(t-butyl)phenyl] iodonium tetra(nonafluoro-t-butoxy)aluminate (abbrev.: iodonium salt or Iod, the cation commonly participating in the photochemical reactions are written as Ar2I+ in the context) and tertiary amine, e.g., ethyl dimethylaminobenzoate (abbrev.: EDB), as three-component PISs to initiate free radical polymerization via the following mechanism [59]:
(r3) PI + photon → PI* (hν)
(r4) PI* + Ar2I+ → PI•+ + Ar•+ Ar2I
(r5) PI•+ + EDB → PI + EDB• +

Real-time Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (RT-FTIR) is the most popular technique to evaluate the efficiency of photopolymerization reactions in terms of the conversions of monomers over time. In particular, the conversions of monomers can be evaluated by the decreases in the vibration peak area (A) of the characteristic bands (e.g., CC bond at ∼1630 cm-1, 4730 cm-1 or ∼6130 cm-1; polyether bond at 1080 cm-1) [60] in the FTIR spectra, indicating the conversions of monomers to polymers. Specifically, the conversions of monomers can be calculated by the following equation [61–63]:
(1) conversion (%) = (A 0 − A
t )/A 0 × 100

where the A0 is the characteristic peak area before irradiation and At is the characteristic peak area after irradiation for t seconds. One of the main factors affecting the rates and final conversions of photopolymerization are the light absorption of the PCs/PIs and their molar extinction coefficients (ε) at the emission wavelengths of light sources. On the other hand, the change in the free energy of electron transfer (ΔGet) of the components in photoinitiating systems (PISs) is also an important parameter; specifically, the electron transfer (ΔG) values were obtained from the ΔGet equations. Here, negative values of the free energy changes associated with electron transfer indicate that the photopolymerization processes are thermodynamically favorable [64].

Currently, there are demands for low-toxic and efficient PCs/PIs. CDs, tiny quasi-zero dimensional nanomaterials in the nanocarbon family with diameters less than 10 nm, have attracted increasing scientific attention due to their unique advantages, including easy preparation from natural materials, low cost, low toxicity, exceptional solubility in aqueous and polar organic solvents, excellent photostability, chemical passivity, and photobleaching resistance [65–72]. Commonly, CDs can be synthesized by two routes: (1) a bottom-up procedure to synthesize CDs from small precursors such as carbohydrates, citrate, and polymer-silica nanocomposites via hydrothermal or solvothermal approaches [65]; (2) a top-down procedure to break down large carbon structures such as graphite, graphene, and carbon nanotubes into CDs using laser ablation, electrochemical techniques, or hydrothermal reduction treatment [73].

Photoluminescent CDs possess remarkable electrochemical redox properties and can work effectively with Iodonium salts; their characteristic absorption spans a broad wavelength range from the UV to the red light [74]. Hence, CDs are expected to be effective photosensitizers or light harvesters with visible light sources in the field of photopolymerization, and CD-based PISs are expected to function with polychromatic light or selected monochromatic excitation by different sources of LEDs. In addition, the biomass-based CDs photosensitizers are recognized as green systems and positively influence the performance of photopolymerization [73,75].

In general, cationic polymerization (CP) can be achieved by interactions between a PI and one or two additives (e.g., bis-(4-t-butylphenyl)iodonium hexafluorophosphate (Ph2I+, it is also marked as Ar2I+ in the context), amine, N-vinylcarbazole, silane) to generate cations or radical cations according to reactions 6–8 [59]. However, the development of efficient visible-light-sensitive cationic PIs is still challenging. In fact, the currently used commercial cationic PIs possess a limited degree of overlap between their light absorption and the emission spectra of the light sources. Therefore, it is important to design new cationic PIs with excellent spectroscopic properties and an efficient initiation process to avoid wasting electrical energy from light sources [76].
(r6) PI → 1PI (hν) and 1PI → 3PI
(r7)
1, 3PI + Ar2I+ → PI•+ + Ar2I•
(r8) PI•+ + Ar2I• → PI•+ + Ar• + Ar-I

In addition, the selection of highly efficient cationic PIs with low environmental impact is another key point for cationic photopolymerization; thus, CDs have attracted considerable attention due to their unique features. In cationic polymerization processes, CDs in the photoexcited state can be easily oxidized by iodonium salt (Iod) through an electron transfer reaction due to their excellent light-absorption ability over a wide spectral range. In contrast, an Iod with strong electron-accepting properties is easily reduced by electron transfer reactions, generating radicals and even decomposing into secondary radicals to promote further reactions [59] In 2021, Tomal et al. [60] used a mixture of CDs with Iod as two-component PISs (Iod/CD systems, 2/0.2 wt%, weight ratios of the monomer used) to induce chain growth cationic polymerization of the vinyl monomer, tri(ethyleneglycol)divinylether (TEGDVE), under two light sources, namely, 365 nm and 405 nm LEDs in air. Here, the polymerization profiles are shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. The Iod/CD PIS seems to be insufficient when exposed to 405 nm LED, even in the cases of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped-CA-CDs) and nitrogen- and sulfur-doped CDs (N, S-doped-CA-CDs) with Iod at the same weight ratio (0.2 wt%), in contrast to the better results achieved with 365 nm irradiation under the same conditions. To accelerate the photopolymerization under irradiation at 405 nm, N-vinylcarbazole (2.0 wt% in monomer) was used as an additive to form three-component PISs, and their polymerization kinetics are shown in Fig. 1c. The addition of N-vinylcarbazole in Iod/CD systems resulted in a higher conversion and faster reaction rate during the cationic polymerization process at a low light intensity, following the photoinitiation cycles involving CA-CDs as PIs for cationic polymerization. A scheme of the photoinitiation mechanisms is shown in Fig. 1d.

Similarly, the photoinitiation ability of Iod/CD PISs was also investigated in the ring-opening cationic photopolymerization (ROP) of the epoxy monomer 3,4-epoxycyclohexylmethyl-3,4-epoxycyclohexane-carboxylate (CADE) at a 365 nm LED in air. Parallel to their above work, Tomal, et al. [60] chose a silane additive, tris(trimethylsilyl)silane, TTMSS (2.0 wt%), to form three-component PISs with Iod/CDs (2.0 wt%/0.2%), which exhibited interesting features such as high reactivity and oxygen-consuming ability in free-radical promoted cationic polymerization (FRPCP). Among the three types of CDs, three-component PISs with the nondoped CDs demonstrated the highest reaction rate, minimum induction time, and sufficient conversion of the epoxy monomer in ring opening cationic photopolymerization. Here, the mechanism of CD-based cationic polymerization in the presence of TTMSS can be described by reactions 9–13:
(r9) CDs → CDs* (hν)
(r10) CDs* + Ar2I+ → CDs•+ + Ar• + ArI
(r11) Ph• + TTMSS → PhH + TTMSS•
(r12) CDs•+ + TTMSS• → CDs + TTMSS+
(r13) TTMSS• + Ar2I+ → TTMSS+ + Ph• + Ph-I

The CD-induced cationic photopolymerization of vinyl monomers was also applied in the rapidly growing 3D printing technique. An N-doped CA-CD/Iod system was dissolved in TEGDVE at a weight ratio of 0.2/2.0 wt% in the vinyl monomer. Then, 0.5 mm-thick 3D patterns “C-DOT” with excellent spatial resolution were attained under a laser source at 405 nm, as shown in Fig. 1e and f [60].

In contrast to the above work, in 2021, Jaiswal et al. [77] utilized femtosecond laser radiation to rapidly prototype all-organic 2D, 3D-functional metastructures of CD composite resin (ACCR) with a submicron resolution of ∼300 nm under two-photon processes (TPPs), where CDs served as both PIs and fluorescent markers in acrylate resins. Here, fluorescent markers were used to stain the microstructures to precisely monitor TPPs induced by CDs. Since the CDs were incorporated into two-photon patternable material system (TPPMS) with preserved optical activity, the microstructures prepared by ACCR showed high-dimensional stability and replication fidelity. This means that the emission intensities of the structures were positively correlated with the concentration of CD loading in ACCR. Following the results, functional gratings were also obtained with subwavelength resolution by the novel method, confirming the possibility of a significant transformation in the rapid prototyping of 2D and 3D metastructures in miniaturized functional devices with better resolution and optical technologies and even biomedical applications.

Radical photopolymerization (RP) is one of the most popular photopolymerization methods based on acrylate or methacrylate monomers. As a general radical photopolymerization process, photoinduced controlled/living radical polymerization (CRP) focuses on radical photopolymerization processes, including nitroxide-mediated photocontrolled/living radical polymerization (photo-NMP) [78], photoinduced reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (photo-RAFT) [79], photoinduced atom transfer radical polymerization (photo-ATRP) [80], and photoinduced free radical polymerization (FRP) [81]. Due to numerous distinct advantages of the polymers obtained from the CRP, e.g., precise average molar masses, various compositions, and well-defined structures, CDs have been introduced into and proven as photosensitizers in controlled/living radical polymerization (CRP) in previous research [64,82].

Recently, CDs have been explored in photoinduced reversible deactivation radical polymerization (photo-RDRP) as PCs/PIs for controlled polymer synthesis [83]. Among the photo-RDRPs, photoinduced reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (photo-RAFT) has been fully discussed as an efficient switchable polymerization technique [84,85]. In fact, applicable PC with proper light absorption, oxidation (or reduction) potential for electron transfer, or sufficiently long lifetime of triplet excited state can efficiently deactivate the propagating radical species in the PET-RAFT polymerization [86].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD-induced photoinduced electron transfer reversible addition fragmentation chain-transfer polymerization (PET-RAFT)
ce:section.ce:para: To the best of our knowledge, heteroatom doping is often an effective way to improve the light absorption efficiency of PCs/PIs in the visible-light region. To date, various heteroatoms, e.g., N, S, P, B, F, and Si) have been doped into CDs to modify their carbon skeletons and even tune their photochemical properties [87–89]. In 2018, Jiang et al. [90] introduced heteroatom-doped CDs as PCs into PET-RAFT polymerization for the first time. Here, in their innovative works, seven structurally different CDs from CD1–7 containing N, P, and S atom dopants were obtained by a bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis approach. A well-dispersed, quasi-spherical morphology without aggregates was detected for all CDs from TEM images, but a higher degree of graphitization with lattice fringes was observed only for CDs 2 and 7, while the other CDs exhibited a limited degree of graphitization with amorphous carbon cores, as shown in Fig. 2a and b. Moreover, the CDs possessed relatively narrow diameters ranging from 2 nm to 10 nm and could be well dispersed in DMSO (see Fig. 2d), and clear solutions with photoluminescence were obtained under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2e). By conducting PET-RAFT polymerization on methyl methacrylate (MMA) as the monomer, poor monomer conversions and low molecular weights Mn were achieved in the presence of CD2–5 separately, where 4-cyanopentanoic acid dithiobenzoate (CPADB) was utilized as a degenerative chain transfer agent (CTA) in DMSO under 465 nm LED irradiation for 24 h at room temperature. Interestingly, the monomer conversions (57%) and Mn were improved by using P-doped CDs, as shown in Entry 6, instead of N-doped CDs. In particular, the high monomer conversion and Mn of 86% and 18,700 were reached, respectively, and the low dispersity (1.06) was observed for CD7 (doped with sulfur atoms), as shown in Entry 7 in Table 1. Hence, heteroatom doping could be recognized as an effective way to improve the efficiency of CDs in initiating RAFT polymerization, especially for P- and S-doped CDs with poor fluorescence properties, to promote more efficient electron transfer and decrease the usage of CTAs.

In comparison, if the light source was switched from blue to red LED at 635 nm, only inefficient RAFT polymerization was observed for CDs 6 and 7 with low-energy photoactivation, and low conversions (17% for CD6, 19% for CD7) and Mn (2940 for CD6, 3790 for CD7) were obtained. However, when the emission wavelength of the light source was changed to 365 nm, the polymerization initiated by the CD 6 and 7 systems exhibited relatively high conversions (40% for CD6, 62% for CD7) and Mn (8420 for CD6, 13,700 for CD7), indicating that the CDs-induced polymerization could be regarded as an efficient RAFT polymerization compared to the direct initiation of the monomers without CDs, with lower conversion and higher molecular weight distribution (see Entry 8 in Table 1).

An oxidative quenching mechanism was hypothesized to describe the CD-induced RAFT polymerization, as shown in Fig. 2f. A charge transfer reaction occurred between the photoexcited CDs and the RAFT agent under light irradiation where the former acted as a reducer and the latter acted as both an oxidizer and degenerative chain transfer agent (CTA). Furthermore, the reduced RAFT agent also acted as an initiator to generate radicals, leading to chain growth during the polymerization, or concurrently interacting with the oxidized CDs to finish the catalytic cycle. In particular, a higher monomer conversion (∼92%) of MMA compared to the reference experiment (∼62%) was observed by introducing triethylamine (TEA) into CD7 and CPADB, as shown in Entry 10, verifying that the PET efficiency and the charge separation between CDs and the RAFT agent could be promoted by introducing electron donors to scavenge the holes. The multi-functionality of the PET-RAFT polymerization induced by heteroatom doping CDs was also verified by changing MMA to the monomers, methyl acrylate (MA) and n-butyl methacrylate (BA), as shown in Entries 11 and 12 in Table 1.

Furthermore, the CD7 system (S-doped CD, Entry 7) was selected for further studies of photo-RAFT polymerization because of its efficient initiation ability under blue LED irradiation. Fig. 2g shows the linear relationships between ln([M]0/[M]t) and the exposure time in the cases of polymerization initiated by the CD7 system with or without the addition of TEA (Entries 16 and 17 in Table 1, respectively), including their pseudo-first-order polymerization kinetics at a low irradiation intensity of ∼2 mW cm− 2 blue LED (Entries 18 and 19 in Table 1, respectively). As a result, a shorter induction period and faster kinetics of the polymerization process could be achieved in the presence of TEA or with an increase in light intensity, which could promote initiation and chain propagation. In addition, the monomodal distribution of the molecular weight of the obtained poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) shifted with exposure time, as detected from the GPC profiles (see Fig. 2h). Remarkably, a linear increase in Mn and a decrease in Đ (dispersity) were observed with increasing monomer conversion, indicating controlled radical polymerization (see Fig. 2i).

As shown in Fig. 2j, good temporal control was achieved by “ON/OFF” experiments. A linear increase in Mn with exposure time was observed when the light remained “ON”, and then the polymerization stopped in “OFF” mode. The final polymer with narrow dispersity (∼1.09) was obtained after several ON/OFF cycles (see Fig. 2k). In addition, a chain-extension experiment was also successfully performed using the MMA monomer with a high final conversion of ∼83%, while a diblock copolymer with a high Mn (∼ 31,000) and narrow distribution of 1.22 can be seen in Fig. 2l. Surprisingly, PMMA with a chain length of more than 300 units also exhibited a narrower distribution of ∼1.05, indicating the good chain-end fidelity of the monomer induced by the S-doped CD-based system. Finally, a relatively high conversion of 77% for MMA and a monomodal Đ for the product were achieved by RAFT polymerization under natural sunlight irradiation in 15 h under the same conditions (see Fig. 2m and n); thus, the CDs could be regarded as effective activators for photo-RAFT polymerization with good control of the molecular weight and dispersity of the polymer.

Free radical polymerization is a popular process based on acrylate or methacrylate to obtain polymeric materials, owing to its fast process, low energy consumption, room-temperature reactions and water tolerance. Thus, this type of polymerization is widely applicable on an industrial scale. For the free radical photopolymerization (FRP) process, PI may interact with two co-initiators/additives (e.g., Iod, amine) to generate initiating radicals upon light irradiation:
(r14) PI → *PI (hν)
(r15) *PI + Ar2I+ → PI•+ + Ar• + ArI
(r16) *PI + amine →PI•- + amine•+ → PI-H• + amine•(-H)
(r17) PI•+ + amine → PI + amine•+
(r18) PI-H• + Ar2I+ → PI + Ar• + ArI + H+

Previously, CDs have attracted attention for their application in free radical polymerization (FRP). In such a polymerization process based on the photoredox mechanism, CDs excited by a light source act as electron donors, and Iod acts as a strong electron acceptor to conduct an electron transfer process. In such a photoredox system, coinitiators (e.g., tertiary amines with low oxidation potentials) are necessary to interact with the excited CDs as electron donors. In 2021, Kütahya et al. [90] prepared CDs in two patterns comprising aromatic (CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3) or aliphatic moieties (CD-4 and CD-5) from biomass to compare their initiation capabilities under LEDs. Here, aromatic and aliphatic CDs were synthesized by different concentrations of amine via solvothermal and hydrothermal reaction protocols, respectively. The low-cytotoxicity responses for these synthesized CDs with sustainable components have been verified to ensure their use as new green photoinitiating systems. Then, the authors used CDs (CD-1, CD-2, CD-3) and an Iod comprising the (t-C4H9-Ph)2I+ cation with three weak coordinating anions as key functions to solvate CD cations more easily within their lifetime. Similar to the traditional mechanism of FRP, photoexcited CDs (CDs*) could transfer an electron to the iodonium cation, which was reduced, leading to the formation of aryl radicals and oxidized CDs (CD•+). Afterwards, the electron could also reform CD•+, thus, these anions were selected to weaken the latter behavior to receive a higher yield of Ar• and maintain the initiation efficiency. In particular, bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide anion, [(CF3SO2)2N]-, derived from super acids was selected to achieve high reactivity because of its outstanding dissolution (577 g•L-1 in the monomer (tripropylene glycol diacrylate, TPGDA)). As the results, the highest initiation rate of CD/Iod systems was achieved with TPGDA as a monomer in the free radical polymerization under 405 nm LED irradiation, and the corresponding data obtained are shown in the FRP column in Table 2. In addition, free radical polymerization was also initiated by the aforementioned CDs with longer emission wavelengths of light sources, including 525 nm and 660 nm, to expand the availability of CDs in potential applications, owing to more scattering and greater penetration depths induced by longer-wavelength radiation than by shorter wavelengths. In fact, these CDs could sufficiently absorb at the mentioned excitation wavelengths, and studies have proven the possibility of using CDs as photosensitizers in the presence of iodonium cations and anions in free radical photopolymerization. In addition, CD-4 and CD-5 in this work exhibited good reactivity to successfully promote a Cu-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (abbrev.: CuACC) reaction, where a mixture of phenyl acetylene and benzylazide in the presence of the CuBr2/PMDETA complex in DMSO was irradiated with a 405 nm LED for 4 h.

As an attempt of CDs performed on free radical polymerization, Tomal et al.’s [60] group also demonstrated the photopolymerization of trimethylolpropane triacrylate (TMPTA) in the presence of an eco-friendly photoinitiating system without transition metals under oxygen-free conditions by using 0.2 wt% CA-CDs comprising doping CDs (N-doped-CA-CDs, N, S-doped-CA-CDs) and 2.0 wt% Iod under LED irradiation at two wavelengths, 365 nm and 405 nm [59]. Similar to r14-r18, the mechanism of CD-based free radical polymerization in the presence of Iod and MEDA can be described by the following equations:
(r19) CD → *CD (hν)
(r20) *CD + Ar2I+ → CD•+ + Ar• + ArI
(r21) *CD + MEDA →CD•- + MEDA•+ → CD-H• + MEDA•(-H)
(r22) CD•+ + MEDA → CD + MEDA•+
(r23) CD-H• + Ar2I+ → CD + Ar• + ArI + H+

As a result, CDs could react with Iod and amine under LED irradiation at specific wavelengths to initiate FRP at room temperature. Thanks to the good water solubilities of CDs, CDs are also regarded as effective photosensitizers under oxygen conditions and in aquatic environments, the characteristics of the CDs enable their use for the production of hydrogel materials via the CDs/Iod combinations were used as suitable photosensitive initiating systems with good solubility in a water environment, and a hydrogel product with a thickness of 2.5 mm and an excellent optical resolution was obtained by 3D printing. From this perspective, these synthesized CDs were also expected to be specific photosensitizers for the generation of radicals in water using biobased materials under mild reaction conditions.

As mentioned above, the CDs with favorable redox properties could initiate living radical polymerization; thus, it is rational for CDs to reduce Cu(II) in photo-ATRP under the same conditions. Kütahya, C. reported the use of CDs as green PCs in photo-ATRP protocols under irradiation of 405 nm LED, and TEM images of the synthesized CDs with diameters of approximately 5–10 nm from carboxylic sodium cellulose and phytic acid are illustrated in Fig. 3b from left to right, respectively [92]. Before photopolymerization, the biocompatibility of CDs at concentrations below 400 μg/mL was confirmed. No obvious inhibition of cells was observed (see Fig. 3a). The absorption peak of the CDs at approximately 380 nm, covering the blue spectral range, could adapt to the emission wavelengths of blue LEDs. The maximum polymerization rates (Rp max) and final conversions of TPGDA under the initiation of three anion salts and CDs were obtained after the free radical polymerization process activated by 405 and 470 nm LEDs. The results obtained as shown in Table 2 indicated that certain reactivity could be achieved during the polymerization in the presence of the Iod or sulfonium salt with CDs at a similar time, despite the remarkable inhibition time appearing before the polymerization under 470 nm LED. Furthermore, the photoinitiation abilities of the CDs described above were extended to photoinduced ATRP, and a possible mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 3c. Similar to the mechanisms of free radical polymerization, photoexcited CD* was generated from its ground state CD under light irradiation, then [Cu(II)TPMA]Br2 was reduced by CD* , and a Cu(I) complex was formed with electron transfer. Subsequently, the oxidized CD (CD+•) reacts with bromide to form back to the CD to finish the cycle of living radical polymerization.

Kütahya and other authors also prepared solutions of CDs in DMSO with ethyl α-bromo phenylacetate (EBPA) as a two-component PISs, where a CuBr2/TPMA (tris(2-pyridylmethyl)amine) complex was used as a ppm scale catalyst and MMA as a monomer was irradiated at 405 nm under reduced pressure in different concentrations of the reactants. Polymerization without CDs and CuBr2 was also conducted separately for reference (Entries 3 and 4, respectively). As expected, no polymerization occurred in the absence of the CDs, and only a small amount of polymer was obtained without CuBr2 following a typical mechanism of free radical polymerization where the monomer conversion was less than 1%, and a product with a large molecular weight and dispersity was obtained. Hence, ppm-scale amounts of Cu(II) was necessary for the systems used in this work. As shown in Table 3, the polymerization with CDs as Entries 1 and 2 showed a narrower molecular weight and lower dispersity. Moreover, no polymer was obtained with half concentration of CDs under longer irradiation times, as shown in Entry 5.

Furthermore, the polymerization systems in Table 3 were also applied in light on-off experiments by exposure to LED light with repeating cycles by switching the light on for 30 min and off for 30 min. As a result, the polymerization was dependent on irradiation due to the lack of polymerization observed in the dark (Fig. 3d). Similarly, polymerization was also conducted with continuous switching on of the light. In a first-order kinetic process for the monomer, the concentration of radicals in the polymerization system can be regarded as constant in terms of a linear relationship between ln(M0/M) and time during the photopolymerization process in the system with an initial concentration ratio of reactant as MMA/EBPA/CuBr2/TPMA/CDs= 300/1/0.03/0.135/0.3. The molecular weight of the obtained polymer increased with irradiation, and the dispersity remained at approximately 1.5, which was characterized by GPC measurements at each time interval of the polymerization (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the polymer obtained from MMA and styrene as monomers could be used as a macroinitiator, which has been confirmed by chain extension experiments, revealing its chain-end fidelity for the activation of controlled block copolymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinduced ATRP catalyzed by N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In 2021, Hao et al. [83] successfully prepared four N-doped CDs (CD1–4) from different nitrogen sources with average diameters less than 20 nm via a bottom-up hydrothermal approach, and the TEM images of the synthesized CD1–4 are illustrated in Fig. 4a-d, respectively. Specifically, CD1 containing 100% pyridinic N was synthesized with O-phenylenediamine as the precursor, and its maximum UV absorption wavelength was 680 nm. Alternatively, CD2 containing 55% pyridinic N and 45% pyrrolic N with an absorption at 540 nm was synthesized with citric acid monohydrate and ethylenediamine as the precursors, and CD3 containing 73% pyrrolic N and 27% graphitic N with 490 nm absorption was synthesized with citric acid monohydrate and L-tryptophan as the precursors. However, CD4 without an N-dopant was synthesized from only citric acid monohydrate as a precursor, and a maximum absorption at 470 nm was observed (see Fig. 4f).

Similar to Kutahya’s research, MMA was used as the monomer in this work. The N-doped CDs as initiators and the Cu(II)/PMDETA complex as the electron transfer agent with ethyl α-bromoisobutyrate (EBiB) were employed to induce ATRP under irradiation of a 465 nm LED. The final monomer conversion after 10-h photopolymerization showed that CD1 and CD2 can be regarded as potential PIs/PCs for ATRP under irradiation, owing to their high photoinitiation abilities compared to that of CD3 (double bond conversion: ∼60% for CD1, ∼52% for CD2, ∼22% for CD4). In contrast, ATRP could not be initiated in the absence of the N dopant, as shown in the case of CD4. The strong light absorption above 400 nm allowed CD1 and CD2 to be potential PCs for ATRP under blue light. As shown in Fig. 4i, a first-order relationship between ln([M]0/[M]t) and the reaction time was also observed for both CD1 and CD2; besides, narrower molecular weight distributions of the polymer for CD1 and CD2 were obtained (Mw/Mn < 1.3) compared to the high distribution of CD3 (Mw/Mn = 1.52), even lower than that in Kutahya’s research. In addition, the linear relationship of the polymer molecular weight and the monomer conversion for CD1 and CD2 was also obtained, indicating that the polymerization was under control (see Fig. 4j). By analysing the electron transfer mechanism, as shown in Fig. 4h, CD1 exhibited the best photoinitiation capacity among the four CDs, while CD3 and CD4 were regarded as poor initiators for photoinduced ATRP; thus, CD1 was utilized as a PC/PI for further investigations.

Afterward, the polymerization rates of monomers within different solvents were also examined, and a sequence of solvents giving increasing polymerization rates was provided as: MeCN<DMF<DMSO, owing to their distinct polarities. Electron transfer occurred between the CDs and the Cu(II)/PMDETA(N,N,N’,N”,N’’-pentamethyldiethylenetriamine) complex as the quencher, as proven by fluorescence quenching experiments. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peaks of CD1 and CD2 was significantly quenched with the addition of quencher, while no obvious quenching for CD3 and CD4. Considering the closer distance between pyridinic N and the edge of the carbon material compared to different N-doped forms, pyridinic N-doped CDs could possess better photoinitiation activity than pyrrolic N or graphitic N. Specifically, pyridinic N could therefore produce defects on the surface, leading to the exposure of the edge plane to enhance the reactivity of the edge sites. Hence, two other CDs, CD2a and CD2b, with different ratios of pyridinic N were prepared to confirm the importance of pyridinic N in the initiation capacity of CDs for photoinduced ATRP. As shown in Entries 6, 11, and 12 in Table 3, a decreasing tendency for the final conversions from CD1 (∼60%), CD2a (∼49%) to CD2b (∼45%) was observed in the same reaction time, following the decreasing pyridinic N portion in the CDs from CD1 with 100% pyridinic N to CD2a with 45% pyridinic N and CD2b with 35% pyridinic N. This validated that the contents of pyridinic N in CDs played a crucial role in the photoinitiation abilities of CDs through a simple elimination process; however, the changes in the content of pyridinic N in CDs had no obvious effect on the polymerization control, as observed by the Mw/Mn (approximately 1.27) obtained from the prepared polymers in the presence of CD1, CD2a, and CD2b.

In addition, a decrease in the conversion of MMA was observed for the CD1-induced ATRP with different light sources with increasing wavelengths from blue light (465 nm: 2 mW cm-1, conversion ∼60%), to green light (535 nm: 1.5 mW cm-1, conversion ∼46%), and then to red light (630 nm: 1 mW cm-1, conversion ∼45%). All the polymers obtained under different light sources exhibited narrow Mw/Mn ratios of less than 1.3, indicating controlled polymerization. Moreover, a wide light sensitivities of CD1 could be verified by “ON/OFF” experiments with 2 h time intervals, where the system was sequentially irradiated under blue, green, red and white light (see Fig. 4m). In the presence of CD1, high photoactivity of the polymer chain ends was achieved by utilizing PMMA-Br as the macroinitiator, which disappeared completely after the polymerization process. The molecular weight of the polymers increased from 9300 g•mol−1 to 16,100 g mol−1 with a narrow Mw/Mn∼1.26 (see Fig. 4n). Finally, the ATRP of MMA resulted in a 50% conversion upon initiation of CD1 under sunlight for 20 h resulting in a narrow Mw/Mn ∼1.29, indicating good control for CD1-induced ATRP and the potential use of CD1 as an excellent PI/PC under natural light.

In 2022, Luo et al. [93] prepared amorphous P-CDs via the Alder-Longo reaction between furfuraldehyde CDs (F-CDs) and di(1H-pyrrol-2-yl)methane. Here, the F-CDs used as reactants were obtained from the post chemical modification of furfuraldehyde derived from hemicellulose as a sustainable resource on CDs via the solvothermal method (see Fig. 5a). The aldehyde groups decorated on the surfaces of the CDs reacted with dipyrrolomethane and were successfully transformed into a porphyrin pattern. The cross-linked morphology of P-CDs completely differed from the spherical morphology with diameters of 5–7 nm of F-CDs according to TEM images, as shown in Fig. 5b. Markedly, the newly synthesized P-CDs with a broader range of visible absorbance exhibited better initiation capacity than traditional CDs for photopolymerization. In addition, the longer stability (∼2 s) of holes generated from P-CDs than those generated from F-CDs after turning the light source OFF was verified by photocurrent experiments, as shown in Fig. 5c. In addition, P-CDs exhibited an E ox of 0.58 V and an E red of −0.29 V. The free radical polymerization conducted in the presence of P-CDs with Iod as initiators failed under 405 nm LED irradiation; however, P-CDs exhibited a good photoinitiation efficiency for the ATRP polymerization of MMA as a monomer in DMSO, where CuBr2/TPMA or FeBr3/Br− as a metal catalyst and EBPA as an alkyl halide initiator were employed. In addition, polymerization initiated by two porphyrins, P-Ar (4,4′,4′,4′-(porphyrin-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrakis(benzoic acid)) and P-Me (5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-methoxyphenyl)-21H,23H-porphyrin) was also performed upon 405 nm LED irradiation, as shown in Entries 1 and 2, respectively. However, much lower conversions were observed for P-Ar (conversion: ∼9%) and P-Me (conversion: ∼4%) than for P-CD (∼46%) under the same conditions (see Table 4). Interestingly, the obtained polymers for Entries 1–3 after photo-ATRP exhibited similar narrow molecular weight distributions, close to 1.1, indicating the exceptional control of P-CDs. Furthermore, if the monomer was changed to styrene, lower conversion and similar dispersity were achieved, revealing the fact that the substitution of monomer has little effect on molecular weight distribution (see Entry 5 in Table 4).

Although previous works indicated that CDs could be regarded as particular PCs for photo-ATRP, it is preferable to develop metal-free and environmentally friendly ATRP systems. Hence, a metal-free system was also investigated in this work as Entry 4 in Table 4. Unfortunately, a lower monomer conversion (∼25%) and larger dispersity (Đ∼1.63) affected by increasing side reactions such as recombination/disproportionation were obtained compared to Entry 3, as the specific system without copper failed to prevent metal contamination in the polymer. Moreover, CuBr2 was also replaced by FeBr2 with tetrabutyl ammonium bromide, as shown in Entries 8–10 in Table 4. Unfortunately, the FeBr2/TPMA systems at different concentrations exhibited lower monomer conversions and much higher molecular weights and dispersities than the CuBr2/TPMA systems under the same conditions of photo-ATRP polymerization.

The block copolymerizations of PMMA and styrene were performed successfully to verify the end-group fidelity when PS-Br obtained from Entry 5 and PMMA-Br from Entry 3 were used as macroinitiators. As shown in Fig. 5f and g, the polymerization degree of PMMA-b-PS obtained from PS-Br under the initiation of PMMA reached 880, while a value of only 71 was obtained in the case of PS-b-PMMA initiated by PS from PMMA-Br, indicating that PMMA possesses a propagation rate constant 10 times higher than that of PS, while broader dispersities of 1.4 and 1.6 for PMMA-b-PS and PS-b-PMMA were obtained, respectively. Furthermore, chain extension experiments were also successfully performed on the PMMA obtained from the metal-free systems (Entry 11), where the iron-based system acted as a macroinitiator (Entry 8). Finally, temporal control experiments were conducted on the systems shown in Entry 3 by switching the 405 nm LED light on and off. Although a 23% conversion of MMA was achieved from ATRP polymerization under 12 h light irradiation during the on mode, there was no polymerization observed in the off mode (without light exposure), even though it was not available in the next and third on-off mode cycles (see Fig. 5h). Thus, these results suggested that the temporal control of P-CD-induced ATRP failed because of the deactivation that occurred in the reactive centers of the heterogeneous PCs/PIs affected by polymer formation. From this perspective, CDs can be seen as one of the best candidates to mediate ATRP polymerization, where the polymer products exhibited dispersities similar to those of traditional ATRP using Cu(II).

Interestingly, Das et al. [94] reported a single-step synthesis of CDs in a facile and green route from the green herb dill leaves via a hydrothermal method in 2021. These CDs efficiently initiated polymerization of the monomer of norepinephrine (NE) under UV irradiation, and the photopolymerization process was systematically evaluated by three factors: monomer concentration, CD concentration, and UV exposure time. Furthermore, an innovative sandwich-like composite catalyst, MXene/poly(norepinephrine)/copper nanoparticles, was fabricated by this CD-activated photopolymerization, which was regarded as a potential commercial catalyst for MXene-based materials without toxic reagents. Markedly, the as-prepared composite catalyst exhibited good performance in the reduction of aromatic nitro compounds, e.g., 4-NP (4-nitrophenol), with a fast reduction time, and it retained a high conversion of > 91% for 4-NP even after 10 catalytic cycles at ambient temperature. Finally, continuous flow of 4-NP through cellulose filter paper coated with MXene/PNE/Cu nanoparticles was also performed to evaluate its catalytic capacity and provide a new pathway for future applications, such as supercapacitors, electromagnetic defense, membranes, and biomedical sectors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of photopolymerization initiated by CD-based PIs
ce:section.ce:para: Overall, CDs have shown distinct initiation performance for all types of polymerizations under LED light with wavelengths in the UV, blue, red, and IR regions of the spectra, comprising photo-CP, photo-FRP, and photo-ATRP, etc. Owing to the good biocompatibility of CDs, the search for CDs with sustainable ligands from biosources may appear less harmful and more interesting, although their photoredox properties and catalytic activity should remain similar to those of PCs/PIs from organic compounds. Hence, the influences of these CDs are expected to have excellent potential in future biological applications, e.g., cellular morphology and antimicrobial activity. In 2022, Huang et al. [95] prepared 2 kinds of CDs with excellent hydrophilicity allowing dispersion in an aqueous medium via the solid-phase thermal treatment method. CD-1 and CD-2, without or with citric acid as the raw material were employed, respectively. By comparing the two CDs, there are more nitrogen and functional groups, e.g. –COOH, –NH2, and –OH, in CD-2 with a higher urea ratio in the precursors than in CD-1, and the content of –OH in CD-1 was higher than that in CD-2. From their works, low photoinitiation efficiency and photoinitiation performances of the CDs were observed when used as one-component PIs in the polymerization of PEGDA400. However, the theoretical plausibility of efficient redox reactions between excited CDs and tertiary amine coinitiators (TEA or NPG) was proven by fluorescence quenching and calculations of free energy changes (ΔGs). Moreover, the alkyl radicals produced from TEA or NPG were also confirmed by ESR spectra under irradiation of 405 nm LED in nitrogen-saturated water solutions using DMPO as the radical trapper.

In Huang’s work, 14 specific resins (R1–R14) were fabricated by mixing PEGDA400, CDs and other additives comprising solvent and/or coinitiators to study the photoinitiation abilities of CD-1, CD-2 and two commercial PIs (ITX and 2-EA) as references. The resins were irradiated by 405 nm and 450 nm LEDs, and their conversion and Rmax are listed in Table 5. As expected, CD-1 and CD-2 can directly initiate photopolymerization under irradiation of two wavelengths with additives, even if better polymerization performances of R1–R14 were achieved upon irradiation at 405 nm LED compared to the results at 450 nm. However, poor photoinitiation performance was still obtained in one-component PIs involving CDs compared with the commercial PIs. Markedly, it is obvious that CD-2 possesses a higher photoinitiation efficiency than CD-1. Interestingly, both the conversions and Rmax were increased markedly with increasing CD concentration (R1-R3 for CD-1, R4-R6 for CD-2) and light intensity from 40 mW cm−2 to 100 mW cm−2. When tertiary amines were added, resins R7 to R10 achieved polymerization equilibrium within a short time (∼200 s) and higher Rmax than the references or R1-R6, especially R10, which stood at surprising Rmax levels both at 405 nm and 450 nm LEDs, proving that CD-2/NPG functions as a highly efficient two-component PIS (∼703.1 × 10−2 s−1 @405 nm and 112.3 × 10−2 s−1 @450 nm).

As a result, a potential mechanism of the CDs as PIs was established, as shown in Fig. 6a. Similar to general quantum dots, excited CDs in the singlet state were formed upon visible light irradiation. Furthermore, electron–hole pairs were also generated, and both the electrons and holes reacted with water to produce hydroxyl radicals; however, the electrons participated in superoxide radical generation by reducing the oxygen in water, and the holes oxidized water directly. On the other hand, active initiating radicals were generated after hydrogen extraction or decarboxylation processes in the presence of co-initiators (TEA or NPG), which were oxidized (or reduced) by photogenerated electrons or holes. Markedly, better initiation activities were observed for CD/NPG systems than for CD/TEA systems owing to the weaker oxygen inhibition achieved by the decarboxylation reaction. Furthermore, a higher nitrogen content (36%) within the pyridinic and pyrrolic forms contributed to better photoinitiation activity for CD-2 than the lower nitrogen content of CD-1 (17%), which was mainly in the graphitic form. In conclusion, these results revealed that the initiation capacity was determined by the components and structures of CDs.

As carbon materials with remarkable photoluminescent properties, CD-2/NPG systems were also introduced into fluorescence tracing and ion detection to explore broader application prospects. After DLP and extrusive 3D printing, fine 3D structures with bright yellow fluorescence under excitation printed from GelMA (methylacrylated gelatine)/CD-2/NPG resin (Fig. 6b and c) can efficiently support the adhesion and proliferation of L929 cells (Fig. 6e and f). Here, the high biocompatibility of the CDs/NPG combination distributed in the cytoplasm of L929 cells was proven by the stable cell viability observed after 24 h incubation (Fig. 6d). To achieve highly efficient PIs in photopolymerization, additional research on the optimization of the structure, functionality and initiation process of CDs is needed.

In addition, Chandra et al. [96] reported a 3D polyethylene glycol (PEG) microgel system with a small diameter by encapsulating mammalian cells and CDs as pH nanoprobes in the cell microenvironment because their green fluorescence emission could be changed by decreasing the pH. Despite the fact that a commercial PI, Irgacure 2959, was utilized as a PI to activate the photopolymerization, the CDs were more likely to be functionalized fillers to broaden the applications of photoinduced polymerization by adding CDs as a perspective.

Herein, because these CDs were proven to be biocompatible, with low toxicity and better photostability and photobleaching resistance than organic dyes, leading to an easy proliferation of the cells inside the synthesized microgels, the microgel system can be utilized to monitor cellular growth by detecting the changes in cellular microenvironment pH and even disease progression in the future.

In previous literature, fluorescent CD-MIPs with core-shell structures were used for optical sensing by the combination of CDs as core particles with a layer of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [97–101]. For instance, antibodies were replaced by fluorescent MIPs for cell imaging, and this optimization was used to establish a standard fluorescence immuno-imaging protocol to detect hyaluronic acid on keratinocytes and HeLa cells [102,103]. In an innovative work, CD-MIPs were synthesized with glucuronic acid (GlcA) via localized photopolymerization triggered by the weak luminescence of N-doped CDs as an internal light source, which was excited by UV light (see Fig. 7a). Herein, human cervix adenocarcinoma cells (HeLa) and human keratinocytes (HaCaT) were used as models for transformed or healthy cells, and these cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde due to its low background fluorescence. Then, the cells were incubated with CD-MIP, CD-NIP (NIP: nonimprinted polymers) nanocomposites and bare CDs for 90 min after blocking the fixation and reducing nonspecific binding by glycine [104].

In this work, N-doped CDs were also utilized as fluorescent probes for the bioimaging of cells, which was observed by confocal fluorescence microscopy. The obtained images of the CDs exhibited higher fluorescence, both for blue and green particles on the cells incubated with CD-MIPGlcA compared to CD-NIP and bare CD (see Fig. 7b). Moreover, the density of blue and green fluorescent particles on the cells was also quantified by fluorescence image analysis, and higher fluorescence of CD-MIPGlcA on HeLa cells was also detected than on HaCaT cells (see Fig. 7c). As a substructure (epitope) of hyaluronan, glucuronic acid acts as a biomarker for certain cancers; thus, these CD-MIPs with GlcA could be regarded as powerful tools to distinguish tumor and healthy cells. Indeed, this was the first study to use CD-MIPs for biotargeting and bioimaging hyaluronan in human cervix adenocarcinoma (HeLa) cells and human keratinocytes (HaCaT) as models for transformed or healthy cells, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-mediated visible-light photopolymerization: towards emerging photocuring applications
ce:section.ce:para: After discussing the role of CDs as PCs/PIs in visible-light-induced polymerization, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a heterogeneous metal-free and highly functional 2D nanomaterial was also introduced for photopolymerization. Despite its well-known features, such as the facile synthesis process by thermal condensation of nitrogen-rich precursors, low cost, and high chemical/thermal stability [105–107], g-C3N4 can generate free radicals under light with good photocatalytic activity; thus, it can potentially induce radical polymerization of monomers [108,109]. For instance, Kiskan et al. [41] used mesoporous g-C3N4 (denoted mpg-C3N4) with a tertiary amine (TA) to induce FRP under visible light irradiation in 2012. Then, Dadashi-Silab et al. [110] combined mpg-C3N4 with copper(II) species and ligands for RDRP polymerization in 2014. Moreover, in 2017, Fu et al. [111] used g-C3N4/triethanolamine as a PIS without prior deoxygenation to conduct RAFT polymerization of MMA to produce linear polyacrylate and polyacrylamide. Subsequently, g-C3N4 has been reported as PC/PI to induce various photoinduced polymerizations comprising FRP, ATRP, and PET-RAFT polymerizations. Interestingly, the large number of amino groups on the surface could make g-C3N4 a potential nanofiller [112], and even an emulsifier at the same time as a PI. For instance, in 2019, Cao et al. [113] provided a facile and effective route using g-CN without any extra chemical modification as a Pickering emulsifier and PI simultaneously to conduct surfactant-free emulsion photopolymerization (EPP) under visible light irradiation. Markedly, stable PS, PBMA, and PMMA latex particles with relatively uniform sizes could be directly obtained by the cross-linking of monomers in the presence of g-CNs and water under visible light irradiation, where the g-CNs remained at the center of the particles, and the dispersion of small amounts outside the particles was confirmed after polymerization. Moreover, surface-functionalized g-CN and g-CN quantum dots were also introduced into EPP to improve the particle formation and photoluminescent tunability of polymer latexes, respectively, highlighting the potential for the as-prepared nanoparticles to be used in biomedicine or bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible light photopolymerization induced by carbon nitride (g-C3N4) PIs
ce:section.ce:para: In 2021, Parra et al. [114] used urea-based g-C3N4 (U-g-C3N4) as a PI to induce the heterogeneous PET-RAFT polymerization of MMA under 405 nm LED irradiation in the presence of oxygen. With the exception of U-g-C3N4, the PIS was composed of a tertiary amine (tributylamine, TBA) and a chain transfer agent (trithiocarbonate, TTC), and DMSO was also added to the system as a solvent. To investigate their oxygen tolerance, the amount of oxygen in the atmosphere was varied by filling the reactor, a partially filled reactor with a large amount of oxygen (denoted #PF) and a fully filled reactor with a small amount of oxygen (denoted #FF). Remarkably, the authors highlighted the negative impact of excessive tertiary amine compared to TTC on the PET-RAFT polymerization control. The monomer conversions of MMA in DMSO under various conditions and the corresponding calculated parameters, e.g., theoretical and experimental molar masses and dispersities, are shown in Table 6. Since the formation of dead chains by irreversible termination would decrease polymerization control and increase the dispersity, changes in the conversion of polymerization in the presence of oxygen could be discussed by combining the chromatogram observed in SEC analysis (see Fig. 8) with calculated molar masses and dispersities. In control experiments, both #FF1 in a fully filled vial and #PF1 in a partially filled vial were conducted in the absence of TTC, and no conversion was obtained for either #FF1 or #PF1. For #PF2, photo-RAFT polymerization of MMA was performed without oxygen by nitrogen bubbling for 15 min before irradiation in the presence of TTC. Compared with the polymerization in a fully filled vial where a small amount of oxygen existed (#FF2) under the same conditions, sample #PF2 achieved a higher conversion of monomer (55%) and lower dispersity (Đ = 1.03) after 6 h of irradiation (conversion=34%, Đ = 1.16 for #FF2) due to partial radical trapping by dissolved oxygen in DMSO. However, the radicals in #PF3 were completely quenched in the presence of higher amounts, nearly half a vial of oxygen. Furthermore, the TTC/TBA mixture acted as a PIS by successfully inducing polymerization in a non-degassed medium. Even TBA cannot initiate polymerization without the chain transfer agent TTC, both in the fully filled vial (#FF3) and partially filled vial (#PF4) in the presence of oxygen; however, by adding TTC in TBA with equimolar proportions, sample #PF5 with high oxygen content obtained 28% monomer conversion, while #FF4 resulted in a higher conversion of 42%. The polymerization process of #PF6 resulted in a higher conversion of ∼36% with an excess of TBA at a molar ratio of TBA/TTC of 5 due to the acceleration of radical initiators generated by TBA, resulting in a bimolecular coupling mechanism for CTA and the formation of dead chains in irreversible termination.

To conclude, the mechanism of the aforementioned photo-RAFT polymerization in the absence of U-g-C3N4 has been established under the experimental conditions of both TTC and TBA, as shown in Fig. 9a. Without a tertiary amine, a carbon radical could be generated under 405 nm LED irradiation. Specifically, the decomposition of TTC as both CTA and PI, afforded radicals to initiate the chain propagation by a direct dissociation−recombination or a degenerative chain transfer mechanism. In the case of tertiary amines, an electron transfer process occurred between TA and the end group of CTA. More precisely, TA generated an anion radical species (PnSC(S)Z)•−) and an amine radical cation, which generated radicals by the extraction of one hydrogen from another TBA. Here, the radical anion can serve two roles during the second step: 1) serving as a PC to reduce O2 into superoxide (O2 • −), which can react with H2O2 and TA by disproportionation to form H2O; 2) serving as a PI for RAFT polymerization by generating a propagating radical (Pn.) and a negatively charged species (ZC(S)S−) after decomposition.

Interestingly, free radical polymerization can occur under the conditions of #FF5 in the absence of TBA and #FF7 in the presence of TBA, while monomer conversions of ∼25% and 50% were obtained, indicating that TBA could remove oxygen efficiently. In the catalytic system involving U-g-C3N4, no polymerization was observed without TBA as #PF7 in a half vial of oxygen, indicating a weak oxygen elimination capacity of U-gC3N4 alone, opposite to that with a U-g-C3N4/TBA system, which achieved a monomer conversion of 40% in the presence of TBA as #PF9. These polymerizations without TTC comprising #FF5, #FF7, and #PF9 followed the mechanism shown in Fig. 9b, and uncontrolled behaviors were expected due to their wide broad dispersities (Đ > 1.5). In two-component systems composed of U-g-C3N4 (0.175 mg mL−1 for #S9, 0.5 mg mL−1 for #S10, and 1.75 mg mL−1 for #FF6) and TTC in fully filled vials, the monomer conversions increased with increasing U-g-C3N4 concentration, and the highest conversion of ∼63% was obtained in the polymerization of #FF6, which is much higher than the result obtained from #PF8 in partially filled vials (conversion∼31%) and #FF2 without U-g-C3N4. Hence, the U-g-C3N4/TTC system was expected to induce PET-RAFT polymerization efficiently due to the conversion of #PF8 being even higher than that of #PF2 (conversion∼ 55%) under the degassed conditions, which is the best result discussed above. Furthermore, the polymers obtained by the initiation of U-g-C3N4/TTC system comprising #S9, #S9, #FF6, and #PF8 exhibited similar theoretical and experimental molar masses and relatively low dispersities of less than 1.2, indicating excellent polymerization control (see Table 6).

In the polymerization without TTC as CTA, the presence of U-g-C3N4 under light irradiation promoted electron transfer from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) to reduce O2 into O2− and the formation of radical species from the oxidation of electron donors by forming a hole (h+) in the VB, which was proven to act as PIs for free radical polymerization. However, in the polymerization initiated by the U-g-C3N4/TTC system, the excited electron in the CB generated from U-g-C3N4 upon light irradiation also reduced TTC to form ZC(S)S−, which was oxidized by the hole in the valence band as the next step, regenerating the initial neutral CTA end-group, while a radical (Pn) is also generated from TTC to induce controlled RAFT polymerization.

Finally, the three-component system U-g-C3N4/TTC/TBA was also examined to improve the oxygen tolerance capacity of the PCs. Here, the achieved monomer conversion and wide chromatograms obtained by SEC analysis of the produced polymer (Fig. 8b) increased with the enhancement of the molar ratio [TBA]/[TTC] in the partially filled vial (52% for #PF10, 60% for #PF11, 64% for #PF12). Even the monomer conversions obtained by U-g-C3N4/TTC/TBA were much higher than that of #PF8 in the absence of TBA (31%); however, low dispersities were obtained for #PF10 (Đ ∼ 1.05) and #PF11 (Đ ∼ 1.15), while a high dispersity (Đ ∼ 2.10) was achieved for #PF12. The polymerization control seemed to be damaged in the presence of excessive TBA in the presence of U-g-C3N4 because a high dispersity is characteristic of poorly controlled polymer chains. In conclusion, the U-g-C3N4-based system preferred to induce RAFT polymerization in the presence of TTC but free radical polymerization in the absence of TTC.

The mechanism of PET-RAFT polymerization involving the U-g-C3N4/TTC/TBA system was also established, as shown in Fig. 9b. In the first step, an amine radical cation R3N·+ as an electron acceptor was generated from TBA by the oxidation of a hole in the valence band. Then, the radical cation recovered an electron from (ZC(S)S−) or extracted hydrogen from excessive TBA, and an amine radical was generated to initiate the polymerization.

To expand the potential applications of U-g-C3N4-induced PET-RAFT polymerization, PMMA multiblock homopolymers were also prepared by the authors. Here, the PMMA obtained in #PF11 with a molar ratio of [TBA]/[TTC]= 1:1 was used as CTA (denoted b1) to further extend the polymer chain by RAFT-polymerization of the second PMMA block. After successive polymerization by the same process, the conservation of the CTA end group was proven during PET-RAFT polymerization. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the diblock polymer (b1-b2) and triblock copolymer (b1-b2-b3) were obtained until pentablocks (b1-b2-b3-b4-b5), and the monomodal chromatograms with dispersities for all extended PMMA multi-blocks exhibited a high-to-low shift in elution volumes from b1 to b1-b2-b3-b4-b5, and no shoulder was observed at high elution volumes. For the #PF11 system, U-g-C3N4-induced PET-RAFT polymerization was also performed under sunlight and reused to initiate a new PET-RAFT polymerization of MMA several times to evaluate the sustainability of U-g-C3N4. As a result, a high monomer conversion (70%) was achieved under sunlight, and a high dispersity (Đ = 1.4) caused by TTC photolysis under the UV wavelength of sunlight was recorded by a monomodal chromatogram, as shown in Fig. 8d. On the other hand, the repeated reuse process of simple centrifugation, washing by MeOH and drying on U-g-C3N4 had negative effect on its initiation efficiency in the new polymerization performed under oxygen. Briefly, good recyclability of U-g-C3N4 was expected, which could be proven by the narrow dispersities and approximating data between the experimental and theoretical molar masses obtained, as shown in Fig. 8e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of photopolymerization initiated by carbon nitrides as PIs
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to that of CDs, the photoinitiation capability of carbon nitride can be utilized in many potential applications. As mentioned above, g-C3N4 with numerous amino groups acted as good nanofillers with excellent mechanical robustness; thus, it was possible to enhance the mechanical properties of polymers with the presence of g-C3N4 nanosheets in the formation of hydrogels [113,115,116]. Actually, the choice of suitable photosensitive resins and initiating systems soluble in aqueous solution is crucial for hydrogel production. Moreover, the structural and biochemical properties of hydrogels, e.g., high porosity and high permeability to nutrients, oxygen and metabolic products [117], are similar to those of the extracellular matrix (ECM) of most tissues [118]; thus, these distinguished properties make hydrogels attractive for research on photopolymerization in biomedical applications. As water-soluble nanomaterials, PISs based on carbon nitride hold great promise for the production of hydrogel materials under irradiation with LEDs. However, the current carbon nitride-based nanocomposite hydrogels have many disadvantages such as energy-consuming, time-consuming, and requiring expensive cross-linkers due to the high concentration of carbon nitride [119]; thus, it is essential to harmonize the mechanical and optical properties of the hydrogels, e.g., the original transparency with tough and stretchable mechanics, in a green and efficient way. In 2017, Sun et al. [120] prepared a g-C3N4-based self-standing hydrogel from N,N-dimethylacrylamide as a monomer and N,N’-methylenebis(acrylamide) as a crosslinker under white LED irradiation at 25 °C for 10 h. In 2018, Kumru et al. [121] prepared tough high-modulus hydrogels using carbon nitride as the PI and reinforcer via an ethylene glycol cosolvent route. Then, in 2019, they introduced a convenient way to fabricate well-dispersed g-CN precursor colloids by grafting hydroxyl ethyl methacrylate (HEMA) onto g-CN under visible light irradiation [122]. Moreover, Feng et al. [123] initially developed homogeneous hydrogel-elastomer integration materials with both hydrogel properties to adsorb water and elastomer properties after dehydration by free radical polymerization.

To avoid the loss of the hydrogel properties affected by the elastomer properties, in 2021, Yang et al. [124] prepared a functionalized elastomer hydrogel, polyacrylamide (PAM)-CN elastomer hydrogel (abbrev.: PAM-CN ESH), with a 3D interlinked channel structure by using CN-induced radical photopolymerization under irradiation from a xenon (Xe) lamp. Here, acrylamide (AM) monomers in an aqueous g-C3N4 dispersion were propagated into polyacrylamide (PAM), and then the polymer chains were cross-linked by strong hydrogen-bonding interactions into a 3D network structure in the presence of CN. After irradiation, X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiments were performed on PAM-CN ESH, as-prepared CN and PAM separately to examine the composition and structure of the elastomer hydrogel. As seen in Fig. 10a, similar diffraction patterns for PAM-CN ESH and PAM hydrogel were observed without typical peaks of CN, indicating that CN nanosheets can act as crosslinkers and exhibit good dispersion without obvious agglomeration and stacking in PAM-CN ESH 3D networks. Moreover, the good and uniform distribution of CN in networks could also be observed from the scanning electronic microscopy (SEM). The 3D structure with larger pores as channels can be obtained by swelling PAM-CN in water for 1 h (see Fig. 10c) or ethanol/water at a ratio of 1:1 (see Fig. 10d) compared to that of exfoliated CN (see Fig. 10b), bulk CN and PAM hydrogel. In our previous works, g-C3N4 was also used as a PI to activate the acrylate monomer, TA, under a 405 nm LED. The monomer with distinct concentrations of CN, 1% and 2%, was characterized by SEM after irradiation. The SEM images (Fig. 10e) shows that the polymer has a smooth morphology for the polymer sample with 1% CN, opposite to the poorly distributed morphology for the sample with aggregates of carbon cores in 2% CN due to the limited degree of graphitization.

As suggested, the aforementioned PAM-CN ESH possessed potential optical properties, e.g., optical transparency across the full visible region, photoluminescence, enhanced fluorescence blue-emission intensity, high anti-photobleaching capability, and even improved thermal stability, owing to the advantages of the functional cross-linker CN unit. Interestingly, bright blue emission was observed for the PAM-CN ESH-Al3+ solution under UV light irradiation. Thus, the PAM-CN ESH was explored for fluorescence sensing and selectivity for Al3+ detection by examining the fluorescence intensities of PAM-CN ESH, Al3+, PAM, PAM-Al3+ and PAM-CN. As shown in Fig. 10f, the peak of PAM-CN ESH at 430 nm for PAM-CN ESH increased significantly in an Al3+ solution compared to those of PAM-Al3+ and PAM-CN ESH without an Al3+ solution. To explore the selective sensing of Al3+ in other metal ions, the fluorescence enhancements of PAMCN ESH were also investigated in 12 kinds of metal solutions. As shown in Fig. 10g, PAM-CN ESH with all of the other metals exhibited lower fluorescence intensity than Al3+ under the same conditions, even lower than PAM-CN, indicating the specific fluorescence selectivity of PAM-CN ESH towards Al3+ in aqueous solutions. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission intensity of PAM-CN ESH increased linearly with increasing Al3+ concentrations (see Fig. 10h), with a calculated correlation coefficient of 0.9833. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) of the PAM-CN ESH for the Al3+ ion was given (approximately 0.26 mM, much lower than the guideline value of the World Health Organization for drinking water, 7.41 mM for Al3+) by the equation S/N = 3, which established a relationship between the standard deviation of PAM-CN ESH measurement in the absence Al3+ and the slope of the linear equation for detecting Al3+, as shown in Fig. 10i. Compared with reported available sensors and fluorescent probes with low LODs to detect Al3+ ions, as illustrated in Table 7, PAM-CN ESH seems to be a potential candidate for Al3+ detection and monitoring and photoluminescence performance in practical applications due to its specific portability, adaptability and selectivity to water environments. Finally, the sensing mechanism of PAM-CN ESH was also discussed in terms of the HOMO-LUMO gap determined by density functional theory (DFT), and an explanation was provided for the enhancement of the fluorescence emission upon Al3+: enhancement of energy emits in the form of radiation, regardless of the band gap energy promoted by the binding of CN and Al3+ or the change in the HOMO-LUMO energy resulting from the charge transfer of CN at Al3+.

The degree of crosslinking for the 3D networks of PAM-CN potentially affects the mechanical properties, e.g., toughness, stretchability and shape recovery. Commonly, highly porous hydrogels have difficulty achieving tough rigidity and stretchable mechanical properties in practical applications. To examine the mechanical properties of PAM-CN ESH, rheological experiments were performed on the swelling hydrogel in water (denoted PAM-CN ESH) and ethanol/water mixing solvent (denoted PAM-CN ESH-E). Markedly, PAM-CN ESH-E with more elastic features was proven via frequency sweep tests (angular frequency range: 0.1–100 rad∙s-1, see Fig. 10j), rather than PAM-CN ESH owing to its smaller loss factors (0.037, 0.025 and 0.018 for PAM-CN ESH-E at angular frequencies of 1, 10 and 100 rad∙s-1, respectively; 0.102, 0.071 and 0.056 for PAM-CN ESH under the same condition), which was calculated by their storage modulus (G’) and loss modulus (G’’) as the equation, tanδ = G’’/G’. Within further strain sweep tests at a specific angular frequency (10 rad∙s-1), remarkable anti-damage performance was observed for both PAM-CN ESH and PAM-CN ESH-E as determined by the negligible change in their storage modulus over a wide strain range (Fig. 10k). Compared to the PAM-CN ESH, even to the existing CN-based hydrogels (see Table 8), better mechanical properties, e.g., tensile fracture stress (0.2 MPa) and anti-damage performance for PAM-CN ESH-E, were observed from the constant storage modulus before the transition from gel to the quasi-liquid state with the destruction of the gel network, indicating that the crosslinking degree of 3D networks can significantly promote the elastic and anti-damage mechanics of nanocomposites.

In addition, the content of ethanol mixing solvents with water were also adjusted to examine the effects of the swelling behavior of ethanol on the mechanical features of PAM-CN ESH, e.g., toughness and stretchability. Interestingly, reduced fracture strength and deformation for PAM-CN ESH were observed at ethanol/water ratios ranging from 10% to 30% compared to that swelled in water, indicating fragile changes during the swelling process. In contrast, substantial enhancement of the fracture strength was observed with changes in the ethanol/water ratio from 70% to 90%, indicating the increasing toughness and stretchability of PAM-CN ESH during the swelling process (see Fig. 10l). Finally, the excellent elastomer trait of PAM-CN ESH was also verified by its anti-damage property under swelling in ethanol, and the stretch and strain forces and its perfect shape recovery performance were also determined from the complete recovery of PAM-CN ESH-E in ethanol to the original state after removing the strain, in contrast to the fact that PAM-CN ESH in water is unable to recover under the same conditions. as shown by the tensile loading–unloading curves (see Fig. 10m).

In 2019, g-C3N4/metal-organic framework (MOF) composites with a nanoporous structure and pore diameters ranging from 10 to 100 nm were prepared by a solvothermal synthesis between graphene carbon nitride and a MOF [14]. As expected, the composites were used as PCs to optimize the UV-curable coating performance, and the effectively improved photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/MOFs composites was obtained after compounding both in theoretical and practical aspects: 1) a lower forbidden bandwidth (∼2.69 eV) was determined by UV–Vis DRS data analysis for g-C3N4/MOFs compared to that of precomposition (∼3.86 eV); 2) a shorter UV-curable coating time (∼13 min) was achieved after the addition of g-C3N4/MOFs than the one without the composites (∼20 min). This study provided new insight into the introduction of MOFs into CN-induced polymerization. Ideally, many new concepts for designing functional PCs with excellent photocatalytic performance could emerge in the future.

The two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyzed by polymeric carbon nitride (PCN) has attracted much attention; however, its poor activity and selectivity significantly obstructs its principal application, H2O2 production. In 2020, Zhang et al. [135] prepared PCN with heteroatom dopants (S and K)- as model PCs to control the selective ORR reaction via a photocatalytic mechanism of local charge polarization to increase the adsorption of O2.
