As the key feature of this work, the fast production of H2O2 at a millimolar level was achieved by trapping long-lived electrons under the synergistic association of K bridging and S substitution in the polymeric matrix, which could efficiently promote oxygen capture and reduction in the kinetics of the ORR process under visible light irradiation (1.37 mM•h–1 under λ ≥ 420 nm irradiation or 3.41 mM h–1 under simulating solar conditions). Remarkably, the rapidly in situ photogenerated H2O2 produced in this work could also be used as an oxidant for disinfection and environmental remediation, while in-depth insight into the selective control of charge transfer and heteroatom dopants synergistic work for the ORR mechanism has been provided. In 2021, Che et al. [136] synthesized an ultrathin PCN-based PC (denoted TP-PCN) with a hydrophilic, fragmented structure by the terminating polymerization effect of iodide ions as an invisible inhibitor. The newly developed TP-PCN could form a quasi-homogeneous photocatalytic system to enhance O2 activation and reduce the energy barrier of H2O2 generation, furnishing a high H2O2 production rate (3.27 mM•h–1). Here, the specific structure of TP-PCN with abundant edge active sites (AEASs) could promote the O2 adsorption and activation capacity, reducing the energy barrier of H2O2 formation. Similar to Zhang, P.’s work, this article provided a new idea for the optimization of the ORR mechanism by achieving a quasi-homogeneous photocatalytic system and efficiently promoting H2O2 production. Undoubtedly, these works will inspire broader applications, not only for more effective and selective H2O2 production but also for further developments of fuel cells and nanozymes.

As metal-free semiconductors, g-CNs offered a new pathway for semiconductor heterojunctions. In 2021, Essen et al. [137] reported g-CNs for the oxidative photopolymerization of 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) in the presence of oxygen. No polymer precipitation occurred in this process, but conductive and insoluble films and coatings were generated by post-doping. The high selectivity of g-CN oxidation promoted the generation of neutral oligo-EDOT, especially for the formation of hybrid heterojunction material via negative surface charge where the oligo-EDOT phase was in situ doped with g-CN nanosheets in the case of excess g-CN. Owing to the weak van der Waals interaction between the C–N layers in g-CN, it also attracted great attention to enhance the sensitivity of electrochemical/bio sensors for electrical/biological purposes [138,139]. However, its wide bandgap (2.7–2.9 eV) still limit its visible light absorption, thus how to reduce rapid charge recombination, up to enhance the light absorption and photocatalytic capacity is a crucial issue for new design of CN [140]. Undoubtedly, graphitic carbon nitrides, as promising new nanomaterials and effective components of PISs, can provide a new approach for the development of stretchable transparent and wearable soft electronics and biosensors in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Key properties of nanocarbon-based PCs/PIs and their potential applications: development of diversification
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, the utilization of carbon materials as photosensitizers has played a key role in research on visible-light-induced polymerization processes. Previous works demonstrated that the advantages of CDs and CNs-induced photopolymerization without coinitiators hold great promise to replace the present photocatalysts such as organic chromophores, organometallic complexes, even QD-based PCs, which still suffer from low efficiency and poor stability. In summary, nanocarbon-based PIs comprising CDs and CNs are more competitive in some parameters than the traditional organic PIs, which are presented in Table 9. Considering the fact that the high energy and short wavelength of UV (or visible light) may irreversibly damage organic compounds, better thermal and chemical stability of nanocarbon materials offers an excellent opportunity for their use as more efficient PCs/PIs in photopolymerization, photocatalysis, even in organic synthesis compared to the traditional organic PIs/PCs. In another aspect, despite organic PIs have been proved to activate the photopolymerization under natural light, the nanocarbon materials, including nanocomposites, were also used to take advantage of the full spectrum of sunlight in specific photocatalytic systems, benefiting from excellent thermal stability and mechanical attributes of nanomaterials. Moreover, similar to some traditional photocatalysis activated by semiconductors, e.g. TiO2, ZnO, CdS, etc., of which intrinsic electronic structure composed by a filled VB and an empty CB, nanocarbon materials also enable to act as photosensitizers extending the absorption range of photocatalytic systems by providing additional electrons to the VB of semiconductors [141]. In addition, the defects upon surface of the nanocarbon materials could also be passivated or functionalized to become more stable to achieve brighter fluorescence emissions, higher conductivity, higher quantum yields and lifetimes of the charge carriers by the restriction of electron hole pair recombination process [142]. Previously, high quantum yields of CDs over 80% have been reported in the assistance of metallic doping [143], especially, considering their lower toxicity, CDs could also decrease the negative effect for human health, thus they have been chosen to be used as fluorescent labels in the presence of live cells, e.g. vivo bioimaging, nanomedicine, and tissue engineering [144]. On the other hand, the detection of Cu2+, Ag+, and Hg2+ by utilizing CDs for chemical sensing has been reported to avoid the hazardous effect of heavy metals on the environment due to the CDs seem to be less costly, more stable and sensitive [145]. Besides, such metal-free photocatalysts seem to be gradually emerged to be sustainable and efficient materials not only in traditional applications, such as 3D printing and chemical/bio-sensors, but also in other fields such as luminescent solar concentrators [146], CO2 conversion [147], and photodetectors [148].

Similar to CDs, g-CN with narrow band gap was also regarded as a potential PCs/PIs in these years, owing to its rapid light-response, facile preparation, and thermal/chemical stability in numerous applications such as water splitting [149,150], supercapacitors [151], and lithium-ion batteries [152], etc. Unfortunately, the high defect density, the rapid recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, and the low surface area can restrict the photocatalytic efficiency of g-CN. Interestingly, nitrogen linkers or terminals in CN enable to be substituted by oxygen to increase the charge separation and light absorption. Following this route, g-CN has been proved to achieve a significant breakthrough for hydrogen and oxygen production in water splitting [153,154]. Here, the comparison between these distinct materials for their potential applications in various fields are gathered in Table 10.

Interestingly, a four-fold enhancement of electron lifetime was found for CD/CN mixture compared to CN [155]. Although it is hard to give a conclusion that CDs hold better electrochemical capacity than g-CNs, interestingly, CDs fabricated by sonication method were proved to be an electron acceptor in CD/CN mixture, while microwave-based CDs worked as hole acceptor in the same condition. In fact, the difference in structures, especially for CDs with higher ambiguities, provides more selective photophysical/chemical functions. In our view, the development of highly functional materials in the future will undoubtedly be enabled by the further adjustment and enrichment of nanocarbon-based PIs performed in photopolymerization, resulting in a high impact in the fields of polymer chemistry and photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Perspective of nanomaterial-based PIs: towards silicon quantum dot (SiQD)-induced photopolymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Although carbon nanomaterials have exhibited various advantages, such as low toxicity and environmental friendliness, they are also known for disadvantages, e.g., experimental uncertainty of the chemical structures and inaccuracy in the synthesis method, which are key criteria for the selection of multifunctional materials. As a new type of nano semiconducting material, silicon-QDs (SiQDs) with analogous features, e.g., low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, especially certainty in the chemical structures during the manufacturing process, have been widely used in many fields, such as bioimaging, sensors, and photodiodes [157–160]. Because their chemical properties are similar to those of carbon-based PIs/PCs, newly developed silicon-based nanomaterials, e.g., silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), were also introduced to visible-light-induced polymerization [161,162]. In 2020, Wang et al. [163] synthesized near-spherical SiQDs with an average diameter of ∼7.0 ± 0.4 nm and a quantum yield of 5.5% via a hydrothermal approach, and their TEM images are illustrated in Fig. 11a and b. Subsequently, the prepared SiQDs were used to induce PET-RAFT polymerization as PCs under 460 nm LED irradiation (2 mW cm−2) in the presence of CPADB as the chain transfer agent (CTA) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). 41% monomer conversion was achieved after 28 h for MMA. In addition, TEA as an electron donor accelerated the polymerization under the oxidation of free holes, achieving higher monomer conversion (∼50%) compared to that without TEA in the end (Entry 3 in Table 11). As a control experiment, the polymerization process in the absence of SiQDs only reached 5% conversion (Table 11). In contrast, low conversions were obtained when DMSO was changed to other solvents under the same conditions; for instance, only 11% monomer conversion was observed in THF, as shown in Entry 4 in Table 11, and negligible conversion in toluene, as shown in Entry 5 in Table 11. Temporal control of SiQD-induced PET-RAFT polymerization under blue light irradiation was also carried out, and there was no obvious effect observed on the controllability of RAFT polymerization due to the similar initiation efficiencies of photopolymerization in on and off stages as shown in Fig. 11c.

Furthermore, SiQD-catalyzed PET-RAFT polymerization was also adapted to distinct monomers and visible light sources. Here, good controllability on PET-RAFT polymerization by different monomers could be summarized from the results of entries 8 and 9 in Table 11, where the conversions achieved 48% for methylacrylate (MA) and 63% for n-butyl methacrylate (BA) as the monomers under the blue light irradiation after 28 h. However, no monomer conversion was found during the photopolymerization process under green light in the wavelength of 530 nm (Entry 7 in Table 11), while 67% conversion was obtained with broader molecular weight distribution (Mw/Mn =1.33) under white light irradiation (Entry 6 in Table 11). The proposed mechanism of SiQD-catalyzed PET-RAFT polymerization was also given in this work as shown in Fig. 11d. Similar to the carbon dots and other photoinitiators/photocatalysts, the silicon-QDs are excited by light irradiation as the first step, then the photoexcited silicon-QDs* reduce the CTA to initiate RAFT polymerization by formed radicals, which can interact with oxidized QDs·+ and finish the catalytic cycle at last.

As a promising facile way, the silicon-QDs can act as a bridge between substrates and CTA and promote surface-initiated PET-RAFT polymerization. As illustrated in Fig. 11f, CPADB can be connected with silicon-QDs via a condensation reaction between the carboxyl groups of the CPADB and amino groups on the Silicon-QDs. On the other hand, the surfaces of silicon wafers with −OH groups after hydroxylation treatment can connect with Silicon-QDs by electrostatic interaction, and the CPADB could be anchored to the surface of a silicon wafer as a result. The reflection emission intensity of SiQD-coated silicon wafer after the polymerization process under UV light decreased compared to that before the polymerization, but much higher than pure silicon wafer due to the light absorption and reflection that occurred in the polymer layer as shown in Fig. 11e. Moreover, the SiQD-coated silicon wafer can be efficiently recycled and reused after PET-RAFT Polymerization as a photo-stable catalyst, while the PMMA brushes were grafted on the surface of the silicon wafer as expected. In conclusion, SiQD-catalyzed PET-RAFT polymerization showed excellent temporal, which was widely known to be one important advantage of photoinduced polymerization, and the obtained polymers exhibited well-defined molecular weights and narrow polydispersity. In surface-initiated PET-RAFT polymerization, silicon-QDs also played dual roles as polymer−substrate connectors and efficiently recycle PCs in SiQD-coated wafers. Owing to their wide range of light absorption in UV−visible spectra and light emission from near-infrared (NIR) to blue, silicon-QDs are expected to act as low-cost PCs for reduction, decomposition, and selective oxidation reactions to induce PET-RAFT polymerization and even other types of visible-light-induced polymerization in the future. Moreover, an electron donor can be added to promote the photoelectron transfer efficiency, which is predicted to significantly affect SiQD-catalyzed PET-RAFT polymerization. Compared to the carbon-based nanomaterials with more ambiguous chemical structures and vulnerable forms, silicon-QD-based PIs/PCs that adopt certain chemical structures, higher photophysical/photochemical stability, and stronger erosion resistance, are expected to be powerful candidates for PISs, especially considering the extensive microelectronics market as a perspective.

Although numerous traditional photosensitizers based on organic compounds and metallic complexes utilized for visible-light-induced polymerization have been widely reported in literature, attempts to explore “greener” PCs/PIs are also reported by scientists to conduct a high-performance, oxygen-tolerant polymerization, even in the aqueous phase. In this review, carbon-based PCs/PIs with advantages, e.g., tunability, excitation ability at blue/UV light, low energy activation, and even high efficiency in water, were systematically categorized into two separate parts comprising CDs and carbon nitride. Previously, these photosensitizers with good thermal stability, photostability, and tunable solubility have been successfully introduced into various polymerization protocols, comprising CP, PET-RAFT, FRP, and ATRP, according to their distinct photochemical mechanisms. In these studies, carbon-based PISs were applicable for many new emerging applications, such as 3D printing, synthesis of hydrogel materials, and tissue engineering in the presence of cells. Along with the development of quantum PIs as photoinitiators to the next generation, carbon-based PISs play an important role in visible-light-induced polymerization by upgrading their multifunctionality and utilizing them in wider technology applications such as 3D printing, chemical sensing, fabrication of non-metal semiconductors, and synthesis of hydrogel materials. Remarkably, considering their lower toxicity, nanocarbon-based PIs/PCs have been chosen as fluorescent labels in the presence of live cells, e.g. vivo bioimaging, and nanomedicine, etc. In conclusion, nanocarbon materials can be regarded as a real-life bridge between nanomaterials and photopolymerization to provide broader horizons and even expand the knowledge of the whole materials science. Moreover, silicon-QD-based PIs/PCs with certain chemical structures and excellent rigidity are expected to be widely emerged in photopolymerization, even in microelectronics market. And there should be a rapid development in the novel silicon dots with the versatile functionalization in the future.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ke Sun received his master degree in nano engineering in 2015 from the Sungkyunkwan University (The Republic of Korea), and completed his PhD degree at the University of Haute Alsace, Mulhouse (France) during 2018–2021. Now he is an adjunct post-doctoral researcher of Shenzhen Polytechnic and Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). His research interests are focused on the design of novel organic photoinitiators for their efficient photopolymerization process including relevant (photo) chemical mechanisms, and he co-authored 26 publications (international journals) up to now.

Yijun Zhang is currently a postdoctoral researcher at the IS2M-CNRS, Mulhouse (France). His current research interests are focused on the preparation of composite materials based on photosensitive resins via photopolymerization and the associated applications in 3D/4D printing and gas adsorption.

Di Zhu was graduated from Tianjin University in 2015 as Bachelor of Science, and studied Pd(III)-catalyzed ortho-selective C-H activation of substituted amide arene. She acquired her Master’s degree in Chemical Engineering investigating lipid-based drug delivery system from Northeastern University, Boston MA, US in 2017. Right now she moved to the Australian National University and is continuing her PhD program in Prof. Pu Xiao’s group, focusing on naturally derived photoiniators of practical polymerization.

Xiaotong Peng graduated in 2020 with an honours degree in chemistry from the Australian National University. She completed her degree with a study on thiol-ene photopolymerization with visible light irradiation under the supervision of Professor Pu Xiao. At the end of 2020, she started her PhD studies on fabricating functional nanocomposites via photopolymerization approach under the supervision of Professor Pu Xiao. Until today, she has published a number of research articles on topics such as photopolymerization with naturally derived photoinitiators, thiol-ene photopolymerization, and in situ photopolymerization approach to polymer/inorganic composite materials.

Jing Zhang obtained her Bachelor’s and Master’s Degree from the Beijing University of Chemical Technology. Then, she studied at the École Centrale Paris and obtained her PhD in Materials Science. She was a Post-doctoral Fellow at the Institute of Materials Science of Mulhouse, CNRS (France). Then, she moved to Australia (the University of New South Wales, the Australian National University, and the Monash University) and worked as a Research Assistant and then a Research Fellow. She is now UCL Santos Research Fellow at the University of South Australia. Her research interests include photopolymerisation of biomaterials, polymer nanocomposites, and the development of novel photoinitiators under visible light.

Tao Gong completed his PhD degree at the Sungkyunkwan University (The Republic of Korea) in 2015 and then moved to Korea University and Tsinghua University for postdoctoral fellow. In 2020, he joined Institute of Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Shenzhen Polytechnic as associate researcher. His research interests are focused on the design and fabrication of wearable, flexible composite materials and the mechanism of performance control. He has published more than twenty papers in international journals and owns 5 patents.

Ming Ma received his bachelor’s degree from Shandong University in 2010 and PhD from Sungkyunkwan University in 2017. Later, he joined Hong Kong University of Science and Technology as a postdoctoral fellow. In 2018, he joined Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences as an associate professor. Dr. Ma works on the design and fabrication of nanomaterials for catalytic conversion of solar energy and fossil fuels. He has published more than 50 papers on Angewandte Chemie, Journal of the American Chemical Society, etc. He was selected as the 2016 National Scholarship for Outstanding Self-funded International Students and the 2018 Shenzhen Peacock Talent Class C Talent. He has been appointed as the young editorial board of Carbon Neutrality (2022), Rare Metals (2023) and Tungsten (2023).

Pu Xiao worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the Fachhochschule Nordwestschweiz (Switzerland) and the CNRS (France) after obtaining his PhD. He then joined the University of New South Wales as a Lecturer before being appointed Senior Lecturer and then promoted to Associate Professor at the Australian National University. He moved to Chinese Academy of Sciences as a Professor. His research interests include green composite biomaterials, photophysical chemistry, photopolymerization technology, 3D printing, and light-induced responses of materials.
